Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated check verified that all system file permissions, ownership, and group membership on this XCP-ng Dom0 (RHEL 7-based) system match the vendor-provided RPM package values. The scan executed 'rpm -Va' to compare installed file attributes against the RPM database and filtered results for permission (M), user ownership (U), and group ownership (G) deviations. No deviations were found, confirming that system files and commands have not been modified from their original vendor-supplied state. This meets the RHEL 7 STIG requirement (RHEL-07-010010) that file permissions, ownership, and group membership of system files and commands must match vendor values. The XCP-ng Dom0 system maintains file integrity consistent with the CentOS 7-based distribution packages. Finding: Not a Finding.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated check identified system files whose permissions, ownership, or group membership differ from vendor-provided RPM package values on this XCP-ng Dom0 (RHEL 7-based) system. The scan executed 'rpm -Va' and found deviations in the M (permissions), U (user), or G (group) attributes compared to the RPM database. Per RHEL 7 STIG requirement RHEL-07-010010, all system file attributes must match vendor values to prevent unauthorized modification of system binaries and configuration files. Remediation: (1) Identify the package for each affected file: rpm -qf [filename]. (2) Reset user and group ownership: rpm --setugids [packagename]. (3) Reset permissions: rpm --setperms [packagename]. (4) If changes were intentional and documented, obtain ISSO/ISSM approval and document the deviation in the POA&M. The system administrator must investigate each mismatch to determine if unauthorized modification occurred.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated check verified that /etc/pam.d/passwd on this XCP-ng Dom0 (RHEL 7-based) system includes a substack reference to system-auth. This ensures that password changes go through the centralized PAM authentication stack defined in /etc/pam.d/system-auth, providing consistent password policy enforcement including complexity requirements (pam_pwquality), account lockout (pam_faillock), and password history (pam_pwhistory). Finding: Not a Finding.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated check found that /etc/pam.d/passwd on this XCP-ng Dom0 (RHEL 7-based) system does not include a substack reference to system-auth. Without this configuration, password changes may bypass the centralized PAM authentication stack, potentially allowing passwords that do not meet complexity, lockout, or history requirements. Remediation: Add or modify the following line in /etc/pam.d/passwd: password substack system-auth. This ensures all password changes are processed through the system-wide authentication configuration. Finding: Open.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated check verified that /etc/pam.d/system-auth on this XCP-ng Dom0 (RHEL 7-based) system includes pam_pwquality.so with an appropriate retry value (between 1 and 3). This ensures password quality enforcement is active through the PAM stack, requiring new passwords to meet complexity standards before acceptance. The retry parameter limits the number of attempts a user has to provide an acceptable password. Finding: Not a Finding.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated check found that pam_pwquality.so is either not configured in /etc/pam.d/system-auth or has an invalid retry value on this XCP-ng Dom0 (RHEL 7-based) system. Without pam_pwquality enforcement, password changes may not be validated against complexity requirements, allowing weak passwords. Remediation: Add or modify the following line in /etc/pam.d/system-auth: password requisite pam_pwquality.so retry=3. The retry value must be between 1 and 3. Finding: Open.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated check verified that /etc/security/pwquality.conf on this XCP-ng Dom0 (RHEL 7-based) system has ucredit set to a negative value, requiring at least one upper-case character in new passwords. The ucredit parameter in pwquality.conf controls the minimum number of upper-case characters required. A negative value indicates the minimum number of upper-case characters that must be present. Finding: Not a Finding.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated check found that ucredit is either not configured or not set to a negative value in /etc/security/pwquality.conf on this XCP-ng Dom0 (RHEL 7-based) system. Without this setting, password changes may not require upper-case characters, reducing password complexity and making passwords more susceptible to brute-force attacks. Remediation: Add or modify the following line in /etc/security/pwquality.conf: ucredit = -1. This requires at least one upper-case character in new passwords. Finding: Open.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated check verified that the XCP-ng Dom0 (RHEL 7-based) system does not allow accounts with blank or null passwords to authenticate. The scan checked both /etc/pam.d/system-auth and /etc/pam.d/password-auth for the 'nullok' option in PAM authentication configuration. No instances of 'nullok' were found in either file, confirming that PAM is configured to reject authentication attempts from accounts with empty passwords. This meets the RHEL 7 STIG requirement (RHEL-07-010290) that prevents logon with accounts configured with blank or null passwords. The XCP-ng Dom0 PAM configuration enforces password-based authentication for all local accounts. Finding: Not a Finding.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated check found that the XCP-ng Dom0 (RHEL 7-based) system allows accounts with blank or null passwords to authenticate. The scan detected the 'nullok' option in /etc/pam.d/system-auth and/or /etc/pam.d/password-auth, which permits authentication without a password for accounts that have no password set. Per RHEL 7 STIG requirement RHEL-07-010290, the operating system must not allow accounts configured with blank or null passwords. This represents a CAT I (high severity) finding because it could allow unauthorized access to the system. Remediation: (1) Remove all instances of 'nullok' from /etc/pam.d/system-auth. (2) Remove all instances of 'nullok' from /etc/pam.d/password-auth. (3) Verify no accounts exist with empty passwords: awk -F: '($2 == "") {print}' /etc/shadow. (4) Set passwords for any accounts found without one. The system administrator must ensure all accounts have valid passwords.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated check verified that the SSH daemon on this XCP-ng Dom0 (RHEL 7-based) system does not allow authentication using empty passwords. The scan checked /etc/ssh/sshd_config for the PermitEmptyPasswords directive. The setting was confirmed as 'no' (or defaulting to 'no' when not explicitly set), which prevents SSH clients from authenticating with accounts that have no password configured. This meets the RHEL 7 STIG requirement (RHEL-07-010300) that the SSH daemon must not allow authentication using an empty password. SSH is the primary remote access method for XCP-ng Dom0 administration, making this control critical for preventing unauthorized remote access. Finding: Not a Finding.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated check found that the SSH daemon on this XCP-ng Dom0 (RHEL 7-based) system may allow authentication using empty passwords. The scan checked /etc/ssh/sshd_config and found that PermitEmptyPasswords is not set to 'no'. Per RHEL 7 STIG requirement RHEL-07-010300, the SSH daemon must not allow authentication using empty passwords. This is a CAT I finding because SSH is the primary remote administration interface for XCP-ng Dom0, and permitting empty password authentication could allow unauthorized access to the hypervisor. Remediation: (1) Edit /etc/ssh/sshd_config and add or modify: PermitEmptyPasswords no. (2) Restart the SSH service: systemctl restart sshd. (3) Verify the change: grep -i PermitEmptyPasswords /etc/ssh/sshd_config. (4) Ensure no accounts exist with empty passwords. The system administrator must apply this change immediately given the severity.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated STIG check verified the UMASK setting in /etc/login.defs on the XCP-ng Dom0 system. The check ran "grep -i ^UMASK /etc/login.defs" and confirmed that UMASK is set to 077. A UMASK of 077 ensures that newly created files and directories are accessible only by the creating user (owner read/write/execute, no group or other permissions). This is the most restrictive default permission setting and satisfies the RHEL 7 STIG requirement that default permissions must prevent other users from reading or modifying files.

The RHEL 7 STIG requires UMASK to be 077 to ensure newly created files are only accessible by the file owner. A less restrictive UMASK (e.g., 022) allows other users to read files by default, which violates the principle of least privilege. To remediate, edit /etc/login.defs and set: UMASK 077. The system administrator should also verify that no user-level profiles override this setting in /etc/profile or /etc/bashrc.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated check examines /etc/fstab and currently mounted filesystems for removable media entries (such as cdrom, floppy, USB, and removable device types). If removable media mount points are configured, the check verifies that the nosuid mount option is present to prevent setuid binaries on removable media from executing with elevated privileges. On this XCP-ng Dom0 hypervisor system, removable media mounts are typically not configured as the system operates as a headless server. The absence of removable media mount points means this requirement is satisfied.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated check found removable media mount points configured without the nosuid option. This violates the STIG requirement because setuid programs on removable media could be used for privilege escalation. An attacker could place a setuid-root binary on removable media and execute it to gain root access. To remediate, edit /etc/fstab and add the nosuid option to all removable media mount entries. For example, change: /dev/cdrom /mnt/cdrom iso9660 defaults 0 0 to: /dev/cdrom /mnt/cdrom iso9660 nosuid,nodev 0 0. Then remount affected filesystems: mount -o remount /mnt/cdrom.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated check examines /etc/fstab and currently mounted filesystems for removable media entries and verifies the nosuid mount option is configured. This check specifically targets removable media devices including CD/DVD drives, floppy drives, USB storage, and other removable device types. On this XCP-ng Dom0 hypervisor system operating as a headless server, removable media mounts are typically not configured. The absence of removable media mount points satisfies this STIG requirement as there are no media paths that could be exploited with setuid binaries.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated check found removable media mount points configured without the nosuid option. This violates the STIG requirement to prevent setuid binary execution from removable media. Setuid programs on removable media represent a privilege escalation vector. To remediate, add nosuid to all removable media mount entries in /etc/fstab. Example: /dev/sdb1 /media/usb vfat nosuid,nodev,noexec 0 0. Remount affected filesystems after changes: mount -o remount /media/usb. System administrators should also consider using udev rules to automatically apply nosuid when removable media is detected.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated check verifies ownership of the /etc/cron.allow file on the XCP-ng Dom0 system. If /etc/cron.allow exists, it must be owned by root to prevent unauthorized users from modifying the list of users permitted to run cron jobs. If /etc/cron.allow does not exist, the system relies on /etc/cron.deny for access control, and this specific requirement is not applicable. The check uses stat to determine file ownership and confirms root ownership for compliance.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated check found that /etc/cron.allow is not owned by root. This violates the STIG requirement because non-root ownership of cron.allow could allow unauthorized users to modify the approved cron user list, potentially granting themselves or others unauthorized cron access. To remediate, change ownership of the file to root: chown root /etc/cron.allow. Verify the change: stat -c '%U' /etc/cron.allow should return root. Also ensure appropriate permissions (mode 0600) to prevent unauthorized read access to the approved user list.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated check verifies group ownership of /etc/cron.allow on the XCP-ng Dom0 system. If /etc/cron.allow exists, it must be group-owned by root to prevent unauthorized modification of the approved cron user list. The check uses stat to determine group ownership. If /etc/cron.allow does not exist, the system relies on /etc/cron.deny for cron access control and this requirement is satisfied. On this XCP-ng Dom0 hypervisor, the cron.allow file is either absent or properly group-owned by root, meeting the STIG requirement.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated check found that /etc/cron.allow is not group-owned by root. This violates the STIG requirement because non-root group ownership could allow group members to modify the approved cron user list, potentially granting unauthorized cron access. To remediate, change group ownership: chgrp root /etc/cron.allow. Verify with: stat -c '%G' /etc/cron.allow should return root. Also ensure appropriate permissions (mode 0600) to prevent unauthorized access.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated check verifies the kdump (kernel crash dump) service status on the XCP-ng Dom0 system. The check uses systemctl to determine if kdump.service is active and enabled. Kernel core dumps can consume significant disk space and may contain sensitive memory contents. Unless specifically required for debugging purposes and documented with the ISSO, the kdump service should be disabled. On this system, the kdump service is not active, meeting the STIG requirement for disabling unnecessary kernel core dump functionality.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated check found that the kdump service is active on the system. This violates the STIG requirement to disable kernel core dumps unless specifically needed and documented. Active kernel core dumps consume disk space and may expose sensitive memory contents. To remediate, disable the kdump service: systemctl disable kdump.service and systemctl stop kdump.service. If kernel core dumps are required for operational purposes, document the need with the ISSO and retain evidence of approval.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated check verified the /home directory configuration on this XCP-ng Dom0 (RHEL 7-based) system. XCP-ng Dom0 is a hypervisor management domain that typically has no non-privileged interactive user accounts (UID >= 1000). The scan confirmed either that /home is on a separate filesystem or that no interactive users exist, making a separate /home partition unnecessary. This meets RHEL 7 STIG requirement RHEL-07-021310. Finding: Not a Finding.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated check found that interactive user accounts exist on this XCP-ng Dom0 system but /home is not on a separate filesystem. Per RHEL 7 STIG requirement RHEL-07-021310, user home directories must be on a separate partition to prevent users from filling the root filesystem. Remediation: Migrate /home to a separate partition or logical volume. The system administrator must ensure proper filesystem separation.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated check verified that this XCP-ng Dom0 (RHEL 7-based) system implements FIPS-validated cryptography. The scan confirmed that the dracut-fips package is installed, the fips=1 kernel parameter is set in the boot command line, and the runtime FIPS mode is active (/proc/sys/crypto/fips_enabled = 1). This ensures all cryptographic operations use NIST FIPS 140-2 validated algorithms for digital signatures, cryptographic hashing, and data-at-rest protection as required by RHEL 7 STIG requirement RHEL-07-021350. Finding: Not a Finding.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated check found that this XCP-ng Dom0 (RHEL 7-based) system does not fully implement FIPS-validated cryptography. The scan detected that either the dracut-fips package is not installed, the fips=1 kernel parameter is missing from the boot command line, or FIPS mode is not active at runtime. Per RHEL 7 STIG requirement RHEL-07-021350, the operating system must implement NIST FIPS-validated cryptography. Note: XCP-ng Dom0 may not support FIPS mode due to its custom Xen kernel. This is a known compliance gap requiring Vates vendor resolution. Remediation: (1) Install dracut-fips: yum install dracut-fips. (2) Add fips=1 to GRUB_CMDLINE_LINUX in /etc/default/grub. (3) Regenerate GRUB config: grub2-mkconfig -o /boot/grub2/grub.cfg. (4) Rebuild initramfs: dracut -f. (5) Reboot. Contact Vates for XCP-ng FIPS compatibility guidance.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated check found that the file integrity tool is not properly configured to verify ACLs on this XCP-ng Dom0 system. Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated check searches for grub.cfg boot loader configuration files across the filesystem and verifies none exist on removable media paths. Standard boot loader locations (/boot/grub2/grub.cfg, /boot/efi/EFI/redhat/grub.cfg, and XCP-ng specific EFI paths) are considered acceptable. Any grub.cfg found outside these standard locations could indicate a boot loader on removable media. On this XCP-ng Dom0 system, no boot loader configurations were found on removable media paths, meeting the STIG requirement.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated check found grub.cfg boot loader configuration files in non-standard locations that may indicate removable media boot capability. To remediate, remove alternate boot loader configurations from removable media paths. If booting from removable media is operationally required, document the configuration with ISSO approval.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated check verified that the telnet-server package is not installed on this XCP-ng Dom0 (RHEL 7-based) system. The scan executed 'rpm -q telnet-server' and confirmed the package is not present. Telnet provides an unencrypted remote access service that transmits credentials and session data in cleartext, making it unsuitable for DoD environments. Its absence confirms compliance with RHEL 7 STIG requirement RHEL-07-021710. XCP-ng Dom0 uses SSH for all remote administration. Finding: Not a Finding.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated check found that the telnet-server package is installed on this XCP-ng Dom0 (RHEL 7-based) system. Per RHEL 7 STIG requirement RHEL-07-021710, telnet-server must not be installed because it transmits all data including passwords in cleartext. This is a CAT I finding on a hypervisor management domain. Remediation: (1) Remove immediately: yum remove telnet-server. (2) Verify removal: rpm -q telnet-server. (3) Ensure SSH is the sole remote access method. The system administrator must remove this package immediately.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated check verifies that the auditd service is active on the XCP-ng Dom0 system using systemctl is-active auditd.service. The audit daemon is essential for producing audit records that establish what type of events occurred, where they occurred, the source, and the outcome. An active auditd service ensures continuous monitoring of security-relevant events including user actions, system changes, and access attempts on the hypervisor host.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated check found that the auditd service is not active. This violates the STIG requirement for audit record generation. Without auditd, the system cannot produce records establishing event types, locations, sources, and outcomes. To remediate, start and enable the audit service: systemctl start auditd.service and systemctl enable auditd.service. Verify activation: systemctl is-active auditd.service should return active.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated check examines the audit failure mode using auditctl -s on the XCP-ng Dom0 system. The failure mode determines system behavior when audit processing fails. A value of 2 causes system panic (shutdown), while 1 logs the failure to the kernel log. Both values are acceptable per STIG guidance — value 2 provides maximum security by preventing unaudited operations, while value 1 prioritizes availability by continuing operation with failure logging. The system is configured with an acceptable failure mode.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated check found that the audit failure mode is not set to an acceptable value (1 or 2). This violates the STIG requirement for appropriate action on audit processing failure. To remediate, set the failure mode: auditctl -f 2 (for shutdown on failure) or auditctl -f 1 (for logging on failure). Make persistent by adding -f 2 to /etc/audit/rules.d/audit.rules. If availability is an overriding concern, use -f 1 and document the justification with ISSO approval.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated check examines /etc/audisp/plugins.d/au-remote.conf to verify the au-remote plugin is configured for audit log off-loading. Required settings: active=yes, direction=out, path=/sbin/audisp-remote, type=always. Audit log off-loading ensures audit records are preserved on a separate system even if the audited system is compromised. On this XCP-ng Dom0 system, the au-remote plugin configuration is examined for all required parameters.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated check examines /etc/audisp/audispd.conf for the overflow_action setting on the XCP-ng Dom0 system. Acceptable values are syslog, single, or halt. The overflow_action determines what happens when the audisp dispatcher internal queue is full, which could occur during heavy audit traffic or when the remote audit server is unavailable. Proper configuration ensures audit events are not silently dropped when the buffer fills.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated check examines /etc/audit/auditd.conf for the space_left setting and compares it against 25% of the audit log partition size. The audit log location is determined from log_file in auditd.conf, and the partition size is obtained via df. The space_left value should be at least 25% of the partition to trigger notification at the 75% usage threshold. This ensures administrators are alerted before audit storage is exhausted.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated check verified that concurrent session limits are configured on this XCP-ng Dom0 (RHEL 7-based) system. The scan confirmed that the 'hard maxlogins' parameter is set to 10 or fewer in /etc/security/limits.conf or limits.d configuration. This prevents a single account from consuming excessive system resources through too many simultaneous sessions. This meets RHEL 7 STIG requirement RHEL-07-040000. Finding: Not a Finding.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated check found that concurrent session limits are not properly configured on this XCP-ng Dom0 system. The 'maxlogins' setting is either missing or exceeds 10. Per RHEL 7 STIG requirement RHEL-07-040000, concurrent sessions must be limited to 10. Remediation: Add '* hard maxlogins 10' to /etc/security/limits.conf. The system administrator must configure session limits.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated check verified that the SSH daemon on this XCP-ng Dom0 (RHEL 7-based) system is configured to use only the SSHv2 protocol. The scan checked /etc/ssh/sshd_config for the Protocol directive and confirmed that SSHv1 is not enabled. XCP-ng is based on CentOS 7.5+, which defaults to SSHv2 only. SSHv1 has known cryptographic weaknesses and must not be used. This meets RHEL 7 STIG requirement RHEL-07-040390. Finding: Not a Finding.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated check found that the SSH daemon on this XCP-ng Dom0 (RHEL 7-based) system may allow the SSHv1 protocol. Per RHEL 7 STIG requirement RHEL-07-040390, only SSHv2 is permitted. SSHv1 has critical cryptographic vulnerabilities. Remediation: (1) Edit /etc/ssh/sshd_config. (2) Set: Protocol 2. (3) Remove any lines referencing Protocol 1. (4) Restart sshd: systemctl restart sshd. The system administrator must disable SSHv1 immediately.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated check verified that the last logon date and time are displayed upon login on this XCP-ng Dom0 (RHEL 7-based) system. The scan confirmed pam_lastlog is configured in /etc/pam.d/postlogin without the 'silent' option, ensuring users are informed of their last successful logon. This provides accountability awareness and helps detect unauthorized account use. This meets RHEL 7 STIG requirement RHEL-07-040530. Finding: Not a Finding.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated check found that the last logon information is not displayed upon login on this XCP-ng Dom0 system. Either pam_lastlog is missing from /etc/pam.d/postlogin or the 'silent' option suppresses the display. Per RHEL 7 STIG requirement RHEL-07-040530, last logon must be shown. Remediation: Add 'session required pam_lastlog.so showfailed' to /etc/pam.d/postlogin. Remove any 'silent' option. The system administrator must configure logon notification.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated check verified that no .shosts files exist on this XCP-ng Dom0 (RHEL 7-based) system. The scan searched the entire filesystem (with depth limits) for files matching '*.shosts' and found none. The .shosts file provides host-based authentication for rsh-related services, which use unencrypted communications. Their absence confirms compliance with RHEL 7 STIG requirement RHEL-07-040540. Finding: Not a Finding.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated check found .shosts files on this XCP-ng Dom0 (RHEL 7-based) system. Per RHEL 7 STIG requirement RHEL-07-040540, no .shosts files may exist on the system. These files enable host-based authentication for rsh services which transmit data unencrypted. Remediation: Remove all .shosts files found: rm [path]/.shosts. Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated check verified that the vsftpd (FTP server) package is not installed on this XCP-ng Dom0 (RHEL 7-based) system. FTP transmits data and credentials in cleartext and must not be installed on DoD systems unless documented as an operational requirement with the ISSO. Its absence confirms compliance with RHEL 7 STIG requirement RHEL-07-040690. Finding: Not a Finding.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated check found that the vsftpd (FTP server) package is installed on this XCP-ng Dom0 (RHEL 7-based) system. Remediation: (1) If not required: yum remove vsftpd. (2) If operationally required: document the requirement with the ISSO and implement compensating controls (SFTP/FTPS). The system administrator must address this finding.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated check verified that SNMP community strings on this XCP-ng Dom0 (RHEL 7-based) system are not set to default values. The scan checked /etc/snmp/snmpd.conf for the default 'public' and 'private' community strings and found neither in active (non-commented) configuration lines. Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated check found default SNMP community strings on this XCP-ng Dom0 (RHEL 7-based) system. The 'public' and/or 'private' community strings were detected in /etc/snmp/snmpd.conf. Per RHEL 7 STIG requirement RHEL-07-040800, default community strings must be changed to prevent unauthorized SNMP reconnaissance. Remediation: (1) Edit /etc/snmp/snmpd.conf. (2) Replace all 'public' and 'private' community strings with unique organizational values. (3) Restart snmpd: systemctl restart snmpd. Consider using SNMPv3 with authentication and encryption. The system administrator must change default community strings.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated check verified that cryptographic hashes of system files and commands on this XCP-ng Dom0 (RHEL 7-based) system match vendor-provided RPM package values. The scan executed 'rpm -Va --noconfig' and filtered for MD5 hash mismatches (flag position 3 = '5'). Configuration files were excluded as they are expected to change. No hash mismatches were found for system binaries or libraries, confirming file integrity is maintained. This meets RHEL 7 STIG requirement RHEL-07-010020. Finding: Not a Finding.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated check found system files whose cryptographic hashes differ from vendor RPM package values on this XCP-ng Dom0 (RHEL 7-based) system. The scan executed 'rpm -Va --noconfig' and detected MD5 hash mismatches for non-configuration files. Per RHEL 7 STIG requirement RHEL-07-010020, system file hashes must match vendor values to ensure integrity. Hash changes may indicate unauthorized modification, compromise, or corruption. Remediation: (1) Identify the package for each file: rpm -qf [filename]. (2) Reinstall affected packages: yum reinstall [package]. (3) Investigate whether changes were authorized. (4) Review system audit logs for evidence of tampering. The system administrator must investigate and remediate all hash mismatches.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated check verified that an antivirus solution is installed on this XCP-ng Dom0 (RHEL 7-based) system. The scan checked for common antivirus packages including ClamAV, McAfee, and CrowdStrike, and confirmed that at least one endpoint protection solution is present. Antivirus software is required on all DoD systems to detect and prevent malware. This meets RHEL 7 STIG requirement RHEL-07-032000 for virus scan program installation on the hypervisor management domain. Finding: Not a Finding.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated check found no antivirus solution installed on this XCP-ng Dom0 (RHEL 7-based) system. The scan checked for ClamAV, McAfee, and CrowdStrike and found none. Per RHEL 7 STIG requirement RHEL-07-032000, all systems must have an antivirus program installed. The hypervisor management domain (Dom0) has access to all virtual machine resources and must be protected against malware. Remediation: (1) Install an approved antivirus: yum install clamav clamav-update (for ClamAV). (2) Configure automatic signature updates. (3) Enable on-access scanning if supported. (4) Schedule regular full system scans. The ISSO must ensure an approved antivirus solution is deployed on all XCP-ng hosts.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated check verified that no accounts on this XCP-ng Dom0 (RHEL 7-based) system have blank or null passwords in /etc/shadow. The scan used awk to check for empty password fields in the shadow file and found none. All accounts either have a valid password hash or are locked (indicated by '!' or '*' in the password field). This meets RHEL 7 STIG requirement RHEL-07-010291 that prevents accounts with blank passwords from existing on the system. Finding: Not a Finding.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated check found accounts with blank or null passwords in /etc/shadow on this XCP-ng Dom0 (RHEL 7-based) system. Per RHEL 7 STIG requirement RHEL-07-010291, no accounts may have blank passwords as this allows authentication without credentials. This is a CAT I finding on a hypervisor management domain. Remediation: For each affected account: (1) Set a password: passwd [username]. (2) Or lock the account: passwd -l [username]. (3) Verify no blank passwords remain: awk -F: '!$2 {print $1}' /etc/shadow. The system administrator must remediate all blank password accounts immediately.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated check verified that access to the kernel message buffer is restricted on this XCP-ng Dom0 (RHEL 7-based) system. The scan confirmed kernel.dmesg_restrict is set to 1 via sysctl, preventing unprivileged users from reading kernel ring buffer messages via dmesg. This prevents information disclosure of kernel addresses and system internals. This meets RHEL 7 STIG requirement RHEL-07-010375. Finding: Not a Finding.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated check found that the kernel message buffer is not restricted on this XCP-ng Dom0 system. The kernel.dmesg_restrict sysctl parameter is not set to 1. Per RHEL 7 STIG requirement RHEL-07-010375, unprivileged access to dmesg must be restricted. Remediation: (1) Add 'kernel.dmesg_restrict = 1' to /etc/sysctl.conf. (2) Apply: sysctl -p. The system administrator must restrict kernel message buffer access.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.
